   `F ◁ g` is again in `J.W`.* Proof sketch (objectwise/local): `J.W` = locally

   flatness (`Module.Flat.lTensor_preserves_injective_linearMap`), local surjectivity
   from right-exactness of `⊗` (always). Note that an ⊗-invertible object is flat

   for our invertible `M,N,P`.

   The only non-`mapIso` content is the bridge lemma of step 1; everything else is the

53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
157	
158	## Rules
159	
160	### What you CAN do
161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
164	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
165	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
166	
167	### What you MUST do
168	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
169	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
170	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
171	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
172	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
173	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
174	
175	### What you MUST NOT do
176	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
177	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
178	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
179	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
180	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
181	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
182	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
183	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
184	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
185	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
186	
187	## Reading references
188	
189	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
190	
191	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
192	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
193	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
194	
195	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
196	
197	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
198	
199	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
200	
201	Conditions for dispatch:
202	
203	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
204	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
205	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
206	
207	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
208	
209	```
210	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
211	  --name reference-retriever \
212	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
213	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
214	  --write-domain 'references/**'
215	```
216	
217	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
218	
219	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.

For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.

38	
39	### Decision 1: which realization of the invertible-scoped associator
40	
41	The associator nests an inner sheafification inside the presheaf tensor on both sides:
42	`(M⊗N)⊗P = a( a(M.val ⊗ᵖ N.val).val ⊗ᵖ P.val )` where `a = PresheafOfModules.sheafification`.
43	Transporting the presheaf associator `α` needs, besides `a.mapIso(α)`, the
44	**absorption iso** `a(a(A).val ⊗ᵖ B) ≅ a(A ⊗ᵖ B)` (and its mirror) = whiskering-stability
45	of the sheafification localizer `J.W`: `J.W g → J.W (B ◁ g)`.
46	
47	- **Mathlib idiom**. Mathlib's general whiskering-stability of a sheafification localizer
48	  is `CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.W.whiskerLeft`
49	  (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Monoidal`), whose signature **requires
50	  `[MonoidalClosed A]`** plus enriched-hom instances on `Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A`. For invertibility/Picard,
51	  Mathlib instead scopes to a *predicate on objects* — `Module.Invertible R M`
52	  (`Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`) — and derives `Module.Invertible.instFinite`,
53	  `Module.Invertible.instProjective`, hence `Module.Flat.of_projective` (all present), so the
54	  invertible/line-bundle objects are FLAT, and flat-exactness replaces the closed structure.
55	- **Realization (1) — local-trivialization gluing**. To build `(M⊗N)⊗P ≅ M⊗(N⊗P)` by gluing
56	  structure-sheaf associators on a trivialising cover, you must first identify
57	  `((M⊗N)⊗P).restrict W` with the affine triple tensor — i.e. invoke
58	  `tensorObj_restrict_iso`. That lemma is a `sorry`; its residual (H1/H2, file L357–398) is
59	  the strong-monoidal-pushforward / `restrictScalars`-along-ring-iso infrastructure,
60	  **absent from Mathlib and flatness-INDEPENDENT** (H2 is about the open-immersion ring iso
61	  `f.appIso`, not about M,N,P). So scoping M,N,P to invertible does **not** lighten it.
62	  The cited precedent `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial` proves only EXISTENCE of local
63	  trivialisations yet still routes through the same `sorry` — `lean_verify` confirms
64	  `sorryAx` in its axioms. **Gap: divergent-and-wrong** (renamed wall).
65	- **Realization (3) — `J.W.IsMonoidal` on the flat subcategory**. Gating Mathlib's
66	  `CategoryTheory.Sheaf.monoidalCategory` needs a `MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal J.W` instance,
67	  whose `whiskerLeft` field is exactly `GrothendieckTopology.W.whiskerLeft` — **`MonoidalClosed`-
68	  gated**. Even restricted to flat objects, instantiating Mathlib's bundled `W.IsMonoidal`
69	  pulls in the closed structure for ALL objects. **Gap: divergent-with-cost** (the wall, packaged).
70	- **Realization (2) — flat-exactness whiskerLeft (RECOMMENDED)**. Do NOT instantiate the
71	  bundled `W.IsMonoidal`. Prove the two specific absorption-iso *instances* directly:
72	  `a(η_{M⊗N} ◁ P)` is iso (P invertible⇒flat) and the mirror `a(M ▷ η_{N⊗P})` is iso
73	  (M invertible⇒flat), where `η = toSheafify` is the sheafification unit (in `J.W` by
74	  `instIsLocallyInjectiveToSheafify`). The bridge `J.W g → J.W (P ◁ g)` for FLAT P is
75	  elementary: via the `J.WEqualsLocallyBijective` characterisation (`Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective`
76	  + `IsLocallySurjective`), `P ◁ g` stays locally injective by
77	  `Module.Flat.lTensor_preserves_injective_linearMap` (sectionwise) and locally surjective by
78	  right-exactness of `⊗`. **All named ingredients present in Mathlib.** Assemble:
79	  `(M⊗N)⊗P ≅[a(η◁P)] a((M⊗ᵖN)⊗ᵖP) ≅[a.mapIso α] a(M⊗ᵖ(N⊗ᵖP)) ≅[a(M▷η)] M⊗(N⊗P)`.
80	  **Gap: divergent-equivalent → ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** (this is the `Module.Invertible`+flat idiom).
81	- **Verdict**: realization (2) — **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB**; realizations (1) and (3) — reject
82	  (both bottom out in absent Mathlib: (1) in `tensorObj_restrict_iso`'s H1/H2, (3) in
83	  `MonoidalClosed`).
84	
85	### Decision 2: re-scope `lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso` from arbitrary `M,N,P` to invertible/flat
86	
87	- **Mathlib idiom**. `Module.Invertible` is a `Prop` object-predicate; the Picard arithmetic
88	  (`CommRing.Pic.mk_tensor`, `mk_dual`, …) are existence-of-iso facts scoped to invertibles,
89	  never coherence on all modules. The flatness the bridge "needs" is FREE on invertibles
90	  (`Module.Invertible.instProjective` → `Module.Flat.of_projective`).
91	- **Project's path**. Blueprint L536 states the associator for *arbitrary* `M,N,P` with "no
92	  line-bundle, local-freeness or flatness hypothesis required" — which is precisely what forces
93	  the arbitrary-`F` whiskering-stability = `MonoidalClosed` wall (its own `% NOTE` L549–562).
94	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost. The arbitrary statement is unbuildable at the pinned commit; the
95	  consumer `lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup`/`thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj` only ever
96	  associates INVERTIBLE objects.
97	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — re-scope the hypothesis to `IsInvertible M/N/P` (or a
98	  `Module.Flat`-style predicate); drop the "no flatness required" clause.

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the structure-sheaf unit are present as
89	scheme-level objects. Rather than the geometric local-triviality route, the
90	group law is built on \(\otimes\)-\emph{invertibility}: an object is a line
91	object when it admits a tensor inverse, \(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong
92	\mathcal{O}_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), exactly Mathlib's
93	\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom. Under this predicate requirement (3)
94	becomes the three monoidal \emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) ---
95	associator, unitors and braiding --- each obtained cheaply: the unitors and
96	braiding as \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the corresponding
97	presheaf-level isomorphism, and the associator (only needed on the invertible
98	objects the law multiplies) by local trivialisation on a common affine cover,
99	while the inverse is carried by the predicate itself. These
100	assemble the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units group is
101	the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The geometric
102	compatibility isomorphism \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\)
103	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and any separate tensor-inverse lemma are
104	\emph{off} this critical path. The full symmetric monoidal category on
105	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is also \emph{not} required; see
106	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
107	
108	\section{Mathlib API survey}
109	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
110	
111	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
112	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
113	
114	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
115	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
116	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
117	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
118	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
119	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
120	sectionwise tensor product
121	\[
122	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
123	\]
124	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
125	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
126	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
127	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
128	
129	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
130	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
131	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
132	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
133	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
134	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
135	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
136	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
137	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
138	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
139	tensor-product operation
140	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
141	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
142	
143	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
144	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
145	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
146	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
147	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
148	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The data this operation must supply for the group law are the monoidal
149	\emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) --- the associator
150	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the left/right unitors
151	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) and the braiding
152	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The unitors and braiding are obtained by the
153	cheap pattern \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) applied to the corresponding
154	presheaf-level isomorphism on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\); the associator,
155	restricted to the \(\otimes\)-invertible objects the group law actually
156	multiplies, is obtained by local trivialisation on a common affine cover
157	(mirroring \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}). Neither route uses
158	the abstract \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) adjoint, a
159	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure, or a sheafification--tensor absorption
160	isomorphism. Together with the
161	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
162	(which carries the inverse existentially) these assemble the commutative monoid
163	of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units group
164	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The project does \emph{not} require a
165	global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on all quasi-coherent
166	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure:
167	monoidal coherence beyond these three isomorphisms is never consumed by a group
168	law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The
169	open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
170	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is \emph{not} on this critical path and is
171	retained only as an optional supplement.
172	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
173	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
174	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
175	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
176	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
177	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
178	independently of any upstream timing.
179	
180	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
181	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
182	
183	\begin{definition}
184	
185	\leanok
186	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
187	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
188	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
189	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
190	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
191	  \[
192	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
193	  \]
194	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
195	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
196	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
197	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
198	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
199	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
200	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
201	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
202	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
203	  canonical compatibility
204	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
205	     \;\cong\;
206	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
207	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
208	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
209	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
210	
211	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
212	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
213	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
214	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
215	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation

223	\leanok
224	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
225	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
227	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

229	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of

233	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;

235	  \]

264	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under

270	\begin{remark}
271	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
272	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
273	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
274	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
275	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
276	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
277	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
278	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
279	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
280	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
281	  depend on it.
282	
283	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
284	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
285	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
286	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
287	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
288	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
289	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
290	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
291	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
292	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
293	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
294	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
295	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
296	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
297	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
298	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
299	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
300	
301	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
302	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the
303	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
304	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
305	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
306	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
307	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
308	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
309	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
310	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
311	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
312	\end{remark}

314	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
315	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
316	
317	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
318	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
319	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
320	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
321	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
322	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the restriction-compatibility
323	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
324	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
325	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
326	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
327	cover, together with their restriction to the
328	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
329	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
330	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
331	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
332	
333	\begin{lemma}
334	
335	\leanok
336	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
337	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
338	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
339	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
340	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
341	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
342	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
343	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
344	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
345	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
346	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
347	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
348	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
349	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
350	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
351	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
352	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
353	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
354	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
355	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
356	  \[
357	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
358	      \;\;:=\;\;
359	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
360	  \]
361	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
362	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
363	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
364	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
365	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
366	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
367	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
368	\end{lemma}
369	
370	\begin{proof}
371	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
372	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
373	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
374	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
375	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
376	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
377	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
378	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
379	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
380	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
381	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
382	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
383	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
384	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
385	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
386	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
387	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
388	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
389	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
390	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
391	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
392	
393	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
394	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
395	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
396	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
397	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
398	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
399	\end{proof}

401	\begin{lemma}
402	
403	\leanok
404	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
405	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
406	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
407	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
408	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
409	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
410	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
411	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
412	  morphism
413	  \[
414	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
415	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
416	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
417	  \]
418	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
419	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
420	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
421	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
422	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
423	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
424	\end{lemma}
425	
426	\begin{proof}
427	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
428	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
429	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
430	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
431	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
432	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
433	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
434	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
435	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
436	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
437	  isomorphism}
438	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
439	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
440	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
441	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
442	  therefore extension of scalars along
443	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
444	
445	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
446	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
447	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
448	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
449	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
450	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
451	  the abstract pullback functor.
452	
453	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
454	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
455	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
456	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
457	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
458	   \;\cong\;
459	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
460	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
461	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
462	  comparison
463	  \[
464	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
465	      \;\longrightarrow\;
466	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
467	      \;\otimes\;
468	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
469	  \]
470	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
471	
472	  \emph{Step 3 (off the critical path).} Completing this comparison at the
473	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor
474	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
475	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the
476	  pinned commit. The genuine residual decomposes into two Mathlib-absent
477	  presheaf-level ingredients --- a presheaf-level identification
478	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) via
479	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (needing a presheaf-level pushforward
480	  adjunction, only the sheaf version of which exists), and a strong-monoidal
481	  upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the ring isomorphism
482	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). This lemma is therefore \emph{not} on the critical path
483	  for the relative Picard group law: the group law is built from
484	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) and the
485	  coherence isomorphisms
486	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
487	  none of which consume restriction-compatibility. The statement is retained
488	  only as an optional supplement; its full proof is deferred.
489	\end{proof}

491	\begin{lemma}
492	
493	\leanok
494	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
495	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
496	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
497	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

499	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of

769	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
770	  \]
771	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
772	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
773	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
774	     \xrightarrow{\sim}

776	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a

780	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the

788	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
789	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
790	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
791	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine

801	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
802	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
803	
804	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
805	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
806	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
807	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
808	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
809	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
810	design template. The inverse of an invertible object is carried by the predicate
811	itself, so neither the open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
812	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a separate tensor-inverse lemma is on the
813	critical path.

880	\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]

915	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
916	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules

920	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
921	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
922	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
923	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
924	  equivalently, an abelian group. Multiplication is
925	  \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\), well-defined on isomorphism classes

927	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, which transports
928	  \(M \cong M'\) to \(M \otimes_X N \cong M' \otimes_X N\) and symmetrically)

930	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (closure); the unit is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and
931	  associativity, unitality and commutativity are the coherence isomorphisms

933	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, read as
934	  equalities of iso-classes. The inverse of \([M]\) is the class of the tensor
935	  inverse \(N\) with \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) carried existentially
936	  by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), so
937	  every element is a unit. This abelian group is the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\)
938	  (Stacks tag 01CR). It is, equivalently, the group of units of the commutative
939	  monoid of \emph{all} iso-classes; that global monoid is not built here, since
940	  the associator on arbitrary modules is not constructed and the group law
941	  consumes only the objectwise coherence isomorphisms on invertible objects.

943	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\)

949	\begin{proof}
950	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
951	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
952	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
953	  Restrict attention to \(\otimes\)-invertible objects. Closure holds: if
954	  \(M, N\) are \(\otimes\)-invertible with inverses \(M', N'\), then
955	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X (M' \otimes_X N') \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the
956	  coherence isomorphisms, so \(M \otimes_X N\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible.
957	  Well-definedness of \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\) on iso-classes is the
958	  bifunctoriality of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}. The
959	  group axioms are propositions about iso-classes and are discharged by the
960	  coherence isomorphisms, each evaluated on the invertible objects in play:
961	  associativity by \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (whose hypotheses are exactly
962	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility of the three arguments), the unit laws by
963	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity by
964	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}; no pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
965	  invoked, since each axiom asserts only the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism.
966	  Every class \([M]\) has an inverse \([N]\) with
967	  \([M \otimes_X N] = [\mathcal{O}_X]\), namely the tensor inverse carried by
968	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}).
969	  Therefore the iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible modules form an abelian
970	  group --- the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- equivalently the
971	  group of units of the (un-built) commutative monoid of all iso-classes. The
972	  whole assembly follows the Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\)
973	  template.
974	\end{proof}

1059	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1060	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1061	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1062	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1063	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1064	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
1065	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the

1077	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its

1079	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from

\begin{lemma}
[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
  left-whiskered morphism
  \[
    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
  \]
  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.

  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
  \(R(U)\)-linear map
  \[
    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
  \]

  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.

  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
  linear maps (Mathlib
  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).

  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
  The argument is the local-trivialisation glue used for
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, applied to three fixed
  invertible objects.

  Since \(M, N, P\) are \(\otimes\)-invertible they are locally free of rank
  one, so we may pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of
  \(X\) on which all three trivialise simultaneously:
  \(M|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\), \(N|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\),
  and \(P|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). On each \(U_i\), both
  \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)|_{U_i}\) and
  \(\bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)|_{U_i}\) are tensor products of
  free rank-one \(A_i\)-modules, hence again free of rank one and in
  particular isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The standard associator of
  the underlying module tensor product over \(A_i\)
  (Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.TensorProduct.assoc}\)) supplies on each
  \(U_i\) an isomorphism
  \[
    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_{U_i}
      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_{U_i}.
  \]
  On an overlap \(U_i \cap U_j\) the two local isomorphisms differ only by the
  transition data of \(M, N, P\), which are \(\mathcal{O}_X^\times\)-valued
  (units of the structure sheaf), and the module associator is natural in each
  argument; tensoring is functorial, so the local isomorphisms agree on
  overlaps. They therefore glue to a single global isomorphism
  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) in
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).

  This is an objectwise isomorphism for the three fixed invertible objects, not
  a natural associator on a category: no pentagon identity is constructed or
  used. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only the existence of this
  isomorphism, \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so this is all that
  is required. The construction uses no
  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\), no
  sheafification--tensor absorption isomorphism, and no open-immersion
  restriction-compatibility (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
  The associator is built directly from the presheaf-of-modules monoidal data
  by absorbing the two nested sheafifications, with the only non-formal input
  being the flat-whiskering bridge \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.

  \emph{Notation.} Write \(a = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) for the
  sheafification functor and \(M^{\flat}\) for the underlying presheaf of an
  object \(M\), so that the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is
  \(M \otimes_X N = a\bigl(M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}\bigr)\), with
  \(\otimes^{p}\) the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}). Let
  \(\eta = \mathtt{toSheafify}\) be the sheafification unit, the canonical
  morphism \(A \to a(A)^{\flat}\); it lies in the sheafification localizer class
  \(J.W\), being locally injective and locally surjective
  (\(\mathtt{instIsLocallyInjectiveToSheafify}\)). Let \(\alpha\) be the
  associator of the presheaf-of-modules monoidal category,
  \[
    \alpha \;\colon\;
      (M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}) \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}
        \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
      M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} (N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}).
  \]

  \emph{Flatness is free.} Since \(M, N, P\) are \(\otimes\)-invertible they are
  projective, hence flat (Mathlib
  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible} \Rightarrow \mathtt{Projective} \Rightarrow
  \mathtt{Flat}\), via \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.of\_projective}\)). The flatness
  hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} is therefore satisfied by each
  of \(M, N, P\) at no cost.

  \emph{The nested form.} Unfolding the substrate on both sides exposes an inner
  sheafification:
  \[
    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
      \;=\; a\Bigl(\, a\bigl(M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat}
              \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\Bigr),
    \qquad
    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
      \;=\; a\Bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p}
              a\bigl(N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat} \,\Bigr).
  \]
  The required isomorphism is the three-step composite:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item \emph{Absorb the inner sheafification on the left.} The unit
      \(\eta_{M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}}\) lies in \(J.W\); its
      right-whiskering by the flat object \(P\),
      \(\eta_{M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}} \triangleright P^{\flat}\),
      again lies in \(J.W\) by \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (\(P\) flat).
      Applying the sheafification functor \(a\) inverts it:
      \[
        a\Bigl(\, a\bigl(M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat}
              \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\Bigr)
          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
        a\bigl(\, (M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}) \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\bigr).
      \]
    \item \emph{Transport the presheaf associator.} Apply \(a\) to \(\alpha\):
      \[
        a\bigl(\, (M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}) \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\bigr)
          \;\xrightarrow[\ \sim\ ]{\;a.\mathtt{mapIso}\,\alpha\;}\;
        a\bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} (N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}) \,\bigr).
      \]
    \item \emph{Restore the inner sheafification on the right.} The unit
      \(\eta_{N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}}\) lies in \(J.W\); its
      left-whiskering by the flat object \(M\),
      \(M^{\flat} \triangleleft \eta_{N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}}\), again
      lies in \(J.W\) by \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (\(M\) flat). Applying
      \(a\) inverts it:
      \[
        a\bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} (N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}) \,\bigr)
          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
        a\Bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p}
              a\bigl(N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat} \,\Bigr)
          \;=\; M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P).
      \]
  \end{enumerate}
  Composing the three isomorphisms yields
  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim} M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\).
  Only steps 1 and 3 carry non-formal content, and that content is exactly the
  flat-whiskering bridge \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}; step 2 is the image

  \emph{no} open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), and \emph{no} per-object local
  trivialisation or gluing cocycle on an affine cover: the two absorption
  isomorphisms are obtained directly from flatness of the whiskering object via
  \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}, without instantiating any bundled monoidal
  structure on the localizer class.
\end{proof}
